skill.name: azure-monitor-query-java
skill.description: Guidance for using the deprecated Azure Monitor Query SDK for Java to query Log Analytics workspaces and Azure metrics.
skill.summary: Build Java examples for Azure Monitor logs and metrics queries, with migration guidance for newer packages.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution, C2 keyword, weak-crypto, and credential-directory alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, Azure documentation links, and placeholder names. The skill is not malicious, but it legitimately teaches Azure SDK clients that authenticate with DefaultAzureCredential and send queries to Azure Monitor endpoints, so it should publish with a credential and network-use warning.

medium_findings.title: Azure Credential Chain Used in Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples create Azure SDK clients with DefaultAzureCredential. This is expected for Azure Monitor guidance, but generated code may use local Azure CLI, managed identity, or environment credentials, so users should restrict permissions and avoid exposing query results.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential usage is explicit and central to the SDK examples. It is a legitimate pattern, but it can access real Azure identities in the user's environment.

medium_findings.title: Network Queries to Azure Monitor Services
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches users to send Log Analytics and metrics queries to Azure Monitor endpoints. This is intrinsic to the skill, but queries can include resource identifiers, workspace IDs, and operational telemetry.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly configure Azure service endpoints and call query methods. The destinations are legitimate Azure services, not unknown third-party endpoints.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The 46 static external_commands alerts are false positives. The flagged backticks are Markdown code fences and inline package names in documentation, not Ruby shell execution or runnable skill scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation with fenced XML, bash, Java, and plain-text examples. No executable skill file, shell invocation, or user-input command construction is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Azure Endpoints
low_findings.description: The network URL alerts point to Azure migration guides, sovereign cloud endpoints, Maven Central, GitHub, and Microsoft Learn documentation. No evidence found of exfiltration to an attacker-controlled host.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references or official Azure service endpoints. They are not hidden, encoded, or combined with credential transmission logic.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Based Blockers Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The critical credential-directory alert at line 412 is a Maven package URL. The C2 alert at line 327 is a placeholder metric name, and the weak-crypto alerts have no cryptographic context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal documentation text and placeholders at the flagged lines. No command-and-control instruction, credential directory access, or cryptographic implementation is present.

content.value_statement: Java teams need reliable examples for Log Analytics and Azure metrics queries. This skill provides focused Azure Monitor Query SDK patterns and migration guidance for newer packages.
actual_capabilities: Creates Java examples for LogsQueryClient and LogsQueryAsyncClient.
Shows MetricsQueryClient and MetricsClient usage for resource metrics.
Explains workspace, resource, batch, and multi-workspace query patterns.
Includes sovereign cloud endpoint configuration examples.
Covers response structures, partial failures, and HttpResponseException handling.
Highlights migration from azure-monitor-query to newer logs and metrics packages.
limitations: Uses a deprecated Azure package and should not be the first choice for new projects.
Requires valid Azure permissions and configured credentials before examples can run.
Does not inspect or validate a user's live Azure tenant.
Does not replace official Azure SDK migration documentation.

use_cases.title: Add Log Analytics Queries
use_cases.description: Generate Java client setup and query patterns for workspace logs with time intervals and result handling.

use_cases.title: Build Metrics Reporting
use_cases.description: Create examples that query Azure resource metrics with aggregations, dimensions, and response iteration.

use_cases.title: Plan SDK Migration
use_cases.description: Identify deprecated package usage and outline migration work toward azure-monitor-query-logs and azure-monitor-query-metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Logs Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me a Java example that uses LogsQueryClient to query a Log Analytics workspace for the last 24 hours.

prompt_templates.title: Query Azure Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Java example that uses MetricsQueryClient to read average and count metrics from one Azure resource.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Query Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Add robust Java error handling for Azure Monitor logs queries, including partial failures and HTTP response errors.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Java service for azure-monitor-query usage and propose a migration plan to the newer Azure Monitor logs and metrics packages.

output_examples.input: A request for a Log Analytics workspace query in Java.
output_examples.output: A concise Java implementation outline with client creation, workspace ID input, Kusto query text, time interval, and row iteration.

output_examples.input: A request to read metrics from an Azure resource.
output_examples.output: A metrics query walkthrough covering resource ID, metric names, aggregation options, and safe handling of time-series values.

output_examples.input: A request to modernize deprecated Azure Monitor Query usage.
output_examples.output: A migration checklist that separates logs and metrics clients, updates dependencies, and calls out testing needs.

best_practices: Use least-privilege Azure identities for all generated examples.
Limit Kusto result size with project, take, top, and narrow time intervals.
Prefer the newer Azure Monitor logs and metrics packages for new Java work.
anti_patterns: Do not paste secrets, tokens, or tenant-specific identifiers into prompts.
Do not run broad Kusto queries without time limits or column projections.
Do not start new projects on the deprecated azure-monitor-query package.

faq.question: Is this skill for new Java projects?
faq.answer: It can help with legacy code, but new projects should prefer azure-monitor-query-logs and azure-monitor-query-metrics.

faq.question: Does it execute queries against my Azure tenant?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Your application runs the queries with your configured Azure credentials.

faq.question: Can it help with Kusto Query Language?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes common Kusto patterns for Log Analytics workspace and resource queries.

faq.question: Does it cover asynchronous clients?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes synchronous and asynchronous client creation patterns.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: The examples use Azure SDK credential patterns, usually DefaultAzureCredential with permissions for the target workspace or resource.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot failed queries?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes partial failure checks and HttpResponseException handling patterns.
